target.title: fix(auth): cooldown inline api key billing failures
target.body:
## Summary
- record provider-level cooldown state for inline `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` credentials
- block literal, env-marker, and env SecretRef inline API keys while provider-level auth/billing cooldown is active
- keep healthy stored auth profiles available as fallback when an inline provider key is cooling down
- preserve inline provider cooldown usage state across auth-store load/merge pruning

Closes #39807.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Inline provider API keys without auth profiles now record billing/auth failures under a provider-level usage id, skip immediate reuse during cooldown, and become available again after cooldown expiry. Env-backed inline keys use the same cooldown gate, while healthy stored profiles for the provider remain available.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout rebased on upstream/main at 5b310a7b27 with branch head 6db13645ab.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx -e '<local inline-api-key cooldown probe using AuthProfileStore, markInlineProviderApiKeyFailure, and getProviderAuthOption>'`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.run-embedded-agent.auth-profile-rotation.e2e.test.ts --reporter=dot`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.run-embedded-agent.auth-profile-rotation.e2e.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the local inline API key cooldown probe:
  ```text
  { keys: [ 'inline-api-key:demo-local' ], until: 1780245179166 }
  threw Inline API key for provider "demo-local" is temporarily disabled after a provider auth/billing failure. Retry after about 1 minute, or switch to a different auth profile/API key.
  ```
  Supplemental checks also passed: agents Vitest shard 3 files / 106 tests, e2e Vitest shard 1 file / 28 tests, oxfmt on touched files, and `git diff --check`.
- **Observed result after fix**: The production auth-store persisted the inline provider cooldown under `inline-api-key:demo-local`, then model auth rejected immediate reuse of that provider's inline key with the expected temporary-disabled auth/billing message. Regression coverage also verifies env-backed inline keys reject during cooldown, assistant/runner no-profile billing paths mark provider-level cooldown, and stored provider profiles still resolve when the inline key is cooling down.
- **What was not tested**: A live paid-provider 402 against a real exhausted account; the local probe exercises production auth-store and model-auth paths without intentionally triggering real billing failures.

## Background

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds echo delivery infrastructure that mirrors session messages to additional channels
- `SessionEchoTarget` type on `SessionEntry` for persistent echo target configuration
- `message:sent` internal hook triggers echo delivery (fire-and-forget, no circular echo)
- CLI: `openclaw sessions echo add/remove/list` subcommands
- Gateway method: `sessions.echo` for programmatic pin/unpin (VituReClaw HUD)

## Motivation

Sessions used across multiple transports (Telegram + VituReClaw glasses + web UI) result in fragmented chat logs. Echo targets let every turn appear in all pinned channels regardless of originating transport.

## Files changed

| File | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/sessions/types.ts` | `SessionEchoTarget` type + `echoTargets` field |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo.ts` | Core echo resolution + delivery |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.ts` | Internal hook registration |
| `src/commands/sessions-echo.ts` | CLI subcommands |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` | `sessions.echo` gateway method |
| `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` | Method registration |
| `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts` | Hook auto-registration at boot |
| `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.ts` | CLI command wiring |

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit test for `resolveEchoTargets` (deduplication, role filtering)
- [ ] Integration test: add echo target via gateway method, send message, verify echo arrives
- [ ] Verify circular echo prevention (echo delivery does not re-trigger hooks)
- [ ] CLI smoke test: add/list/remove echo targets

Supersedes #88386 (closed: branch had unrelated deployment commits).

---

## Proof (manual)

> Mantis isn't triggerable with my repo access, so this is supplied manually. Ran this branch live on a real Telegram bot (Windows, real session store). Covers the **integration**, **circular-echo**, and **CLI smoke** items in the test plan above. Telegram chat id redacted as `<chat>`.

**Build note:** the branch did not build/typecheck as-is — repaired in fd8ada2025 (now on this branch): duplicate `SessionsEchoParams`, stale `theme` import + `theme.bold`, `echoTargets` missing from the reserved slot-key list, and a wrong `.find`/`.filter` param annotation (`threadId` is `string|number`). `tsgo:core` clean after.

**1. CLI `add` / `list` / `remove`** (real store, net-zero):
```
$ openclaw sessions echo add  --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237 --label echo-proof
  Added echo target: telegram -> <chat> (echo-proof)
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --json
  { "echoTargets":[{ "channel":"telegram","to":"<chat>","threadId":"26237","label":"echo-proof","addedAt":… }] }
$ openclaw sessions echo remove --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237
  Removed echo target: telegram -> <chat>
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67
  No echo targets configured for this session.
```
Raw store reads confirmed `echoTargets: null → […] → null`.

**2. Live cross-channel mirror** (source thread 67 → pinned thread 26237):
```
[telegram] Inbound message …direct, 12 chars                            ← user message in thread 67
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36822 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed to pinned thread
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36824 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed
```
Echo fires off `message:received`, independent of the agent turn.

**3. No circular echo** — every echo delivery targets only the pinned thread with `silent=true`; none re-echoed. Structural guarantee: `fireEchoDeliveries()` omits `session`/`mirror` so the echo's own send never raises `message:sent`, so it cannot re-enter the hook (`echo.ts`).

---

## Update — surface-agnostic echo + live web→Telegram proof

Since the last review:
- **`sessions.echo.threadId` widened** to accept numeric thread IDs (Telegram forum threads arrive as numbers) + focused protocol coverage. (`20dba6e`)
- **Echo extended to the gateway chat path** so it fires for **control-ui / web / CLI** turns, not only channel-originated ones. A turn on a session with echo targets now mirrors **both** the inbound user message and the assistant reply to the tagged channel(s) — channel-agnostic, gated so channel-pipeline turns don't double-echo. (`73b6c75e` + the existing inline assistant-echo)

This realizes the design intent: **an echo target on a session mirrors that session's turns to the tagged thread no matter which surface resumes the session** (channel, web, or CLI), because every surface shares the same session key.

### Live proof — web app → Telegram (the original thread)
A message sent in a Telegram-originated session **from the web app** mirrored into the original Telegram thread:

![web to telegram echo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/echo88815-proof/echo-web-to-telegram-88815.png)

Gateway log (chat id redacted) — `echo-to-thread: 0 → 2` from one webchat turn:
```
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37041 threadId=67 silent=true   <- user message   (rendered "[via webchat]")
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37042 threadId=67 silent=true   <- assistant reply (rendered "[echo]")
cli turn ... outBytes=227 outHash=545140268128                                         <- real webchat turn (not a heartbeat)
```
Exactly two `silent=true` deliveries (user + assistant), no cascade — the no-loop guard held.

target.title: fix(auth): cooldown inline api key billing failures
target.body:
## Summary
- record provider-level cooldown state for inline `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` credentials
- block literal, env-marker, and env SecretRef inline API keys while provider-level auth/billing cooldown is active
- keep healthy stored auth profiles available as fallback when an inline provider key is cooling down
- preserve inline provider cooldown usage state across auth-store load/merge pruning

Closes #39807.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Inline provider API keys without auth profiles now record billing/auth failures under a provider-level usage id, skip immediate reuse during cooldown, and become available again after cooldown expiry. Env-backed inline keys use the same cooldown gate, while healthy stored profiles for the provider remain available.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout rebased on upstream/main at 5b310a7b27 with branch head 6db13645ab.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx -e '<local inline-api-key cooldown probe using AuthProfileStore, markInlineProviderApiKeyFailure, and getProviderAuthOption>'`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.run-embedded-agent.auth-profile-rotation.e2e.test.ts --reporter=dot`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.run-embedded-agent.auth-profile-rotation.e2e.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the local inline API key cooldown probe:
  ```text
  { keys: [ 'inline-api-key:demo-local' ], until: 1780245179166 }
  threw Inline API key for provider "demo-local" is temporarily disabled after a provider auth/billing failure. Retry after about 1 minute, or switch to a different auth profile/API key.
  ```
  Supplemental checks also passed: agents Vitest shard 3 files / 106 tests, e2e Vitest shard 1 file / 28 tests, oxfmt on touched files, and `git diff --check`.
- **Observed result after fix**: The production auth-store persisted the inline provider cooldown under `inline-api-key:demo-local`, then model auth rejected immediate reuse of that provider's inline key with the expected temporary-disabled auth/billing message. Regression coverage also verifies env-backed inline keys reject during cooldown, assistant/runner no-profile billing paths mark provider-level cooldown, and stored provider profiles still resolve when the inline key is cooling down.
- **What was not tested**: A live paid-provider 402 against a real exhausted account; the local probe exercises production auth-store and model-auth paths without intentionally triggering real billing failures.

## Background
This replaces the closed polluted PR #80482 with the same intended auth-runtime fix rebuilt as a clean branch from current upstream/main.

target.title: test(policy): add config coverage report
target.body:
## Summary

This is the final policy-series maintainer follow-up. It adds an internal, report-only coverage inventory for the OpenClaw config fields that Policy currently watches for conformance or attestation drift.

- adds `pnpm policy:config-coverage`
- keeps the tool internal to repo maintainers, not an `openclaw policy` user command
- classifies monitored config leaf paths as observed by the policy surface
- supports `--check`, `--json`, and `--show-covered` for policy maintainer workflows

Default mode is intentionally report-only so ordinary config PRs do not have to update Policy. Policy maintainers can run `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check` when they want unclassified or stale monitored paths to fail locally.

## Stack note

This branch is currently opened against `main` for GitHub visibility, but the reviewable change is the final commit:

- `66eb73dbb19 test(policy): add config coverage report`

It is stacked after the active policy line, including #85572, #85744, #86768, #87056, and #87074.

## Verification

- `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check`
- `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check --json`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 package.json scripts/check-policy-config-coverage.ts scripts/lib/policy-config-coverage.jsonc`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.scripts.json scripts/check-policy-config-coverage.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `~/.codex/skills/codex-review/scripts/codex-review --mode local`

- **Echo extended to the gateway chat path** so it fires for **control-ui / web / CLI** turns, not only channel-originated ones. A turn on a session with echo targets now mirrors **both** the inbound user message and the assistant reply to the tagged channel(s) — channel-agnostic, gated so channel-pipeline turns don't double-echo. (`73b6c75e` + the existing inline assistant-echo)

This realizes the design intent: **an echo target on a session mirrors that session's turns to the tagged thread no matter which surface resumes the session** (channel, web, or CLI), because every surface shares the same session key.

### Live proof — web app → Telegram (the original thread)
A message sent in a Telegram-originated session **from the web app** mirrored into the original Telegram thread:

![web to telegram echo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/echo88815-proof/echo-web-to-telegram-88815.png)

Gateway log (chat id redacted) — `echo-to-thread: 0 → 2` from one webchat turn:
```
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37041 threadId=67 silent=true   <- user message   (rendered "[via webchat]")
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37042 threadId=67 silent=true   <- assistant reply (rendered "[echo]")
cli turn ... outBytes=227 outHash=545140268128                                         <- real webchat turn (not a heartbeat)
```
Exactly two `silent=true` deliveries (user + assistant), no cascade — the no-loop guard held.

target.title: Harden node exec approval precheck env [AI]
target.body:
AI-assisted: Yes

## Summary

- Problem: node-host exec approval prechecks could analyze a command with the gateway environment even though the forwarded `system.run` request only carries node-targeted env overrides.
- Why it matters: bare command allowlist decisions should not depend on PATH resolution from a host that will not execute the command.
- What changed: node approval prechecks now build analysis input from the forwarded node env and clear PATH/Path fallback so gateway-local PATH cannot satisfy the precheck.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): the forwarded `system.run` payload, approval binding format, and node-side execution policy are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: provided separately by maintainers
- [x] This PR addresses a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: node-host approval prechecks no longer suppress approval based on gateway-local PATH resolution.
- Real environment tested: Not run in this metadata pass.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Not run in this metadata pass.
- Evidence after fix: Unit regression coverage was added in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`.
- Observed result after fix: Not runtime-verified in this metadata pass.
- What was not tested: real paired-node execution, broad changed checks, and cross-platform runs.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): N/A

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the node-host gateway precheck used the request env assembled on the gateway instead of the env overrides that are actually forwarded to the node.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression case proving that a gateway PATH match cannot suppress a node approval prompt when no PATH is forwarded.
- Contributing context (if known): node execution intentionally blocks PATH overrides and resolves bare commands with the node host process environment.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway PATH must not satisfy node allowlist prechecks when forwarded node env is undefined, and forwarded node env overrides are used when present.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to `executeNodeHostCommand` approval analysis before `node.invoke system.run` is forwarded.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Node-host commands may ask for approval in cases that previously depended on gateway-local PATH analysis. No config migration is required.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
node exec request -> gateway env precheck -> node run with node env

After:
node exec request -> forwarded-env precheck with no gateway PATH fallback -> node run with node env
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: command approval precheck behavior is stricter for node-host bare command resolution; forwarded execution payloads and approval binding remain unchanged.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Not run in this metadata pass
- Runtime/container: Not run in this metadata pass
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): node-host exec
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.exec.host=node`, allowlist/on-miss style policy

### Steps

1. Configure a node-host exec path with node approval prechecks enabled.
2. Run a bare command whose gateway PATH would resolve to an allowlisted path while no PATH override is forwarded to the node.
3. Confirm approval is required instead of being skipped by gateway PATH analysis.

### Expected

- Approval is required unless the node-forwarded env can satisfy the precheck without gateway PATH fallback.

### Actual

- Not runtime-verified in this metadata pass.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

No external artifact attached in this metadata pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed changed-file summary and regression intent for node approval env handling.
- Edge cases checked: forwarded env present, forwarded env absent, gateway PATH not reused for precheck matching.
- What you did not verify: local test execution, real node-host execution, broad CI gates.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Some node-host bare command allowlist entries may request approval more often when the gateway cannot validate node PATH.
  - Mitigation: forwarded node env overrides are still honored for precheck analysis, and explicit approval flow remains available.

target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolate QMD mcporter daemon/config state per agent by writing an agent-scoped mcporter config and passing it to mcporter calls.
- Preserve the configured mcporter server definition instead of replacing custom/remote server config with a hard-coded local `qmd mcp` entry.
- Keep QMD model/env settings flowing only through the generated stdio server entry, while stripping QMD-scoped XDG env from mcporter's own process environment.
- Tolerate mcporter daemon log lines before JSON responses so warm daemon calls do not break memory search parsing.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving a configured stdio `qmd` server even when mcporter stores the command as an absolute executable path.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node `v24.15.0`, `mcporter 0.10.2`, `qmd 2.1.0`, and the configured mcporter `qmd` stdio server whose command is an absolute qmd binary path. Home/state/workspace paths are redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
node --version
mcporter --version
qmd --version
mcporter config get qmd --json
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm build
node --input-type=module '<create QmdMemoryManager in status mode for agents hex/trump, call ensureMcporterConfig(), read generated mcporter.json, print redacted command/env summary>'
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output, redacted:

```text
node --version -> v24.15.0
mcporter --version -> 0.10.2
qmd --version -> qmd 2.1.0 (bab86d5)

mcporter config get qmd --json
{
  "name": "qmd",
  "source": { "kind": "local", "path": "~/.mcporter/mcporter.json" },
  "transport": "stdio",
  "command": "~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd",
  "args": ["mcp"]
}

Focused tests:
- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts: 132 tests passed
- src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts: 30 tests passed

Build:
- pnpm build completed successfully

Runtime classifier/config proof:
node=v24.15.0
mcporterConfig.command=~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd
mcporterConfig.args=["mcp"]
{"agentId":"hex","mode":"generated","configPath":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json","imports":[],"command":"~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd","args":["mcp"],"xdgConfigHome":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config","qmdConfigDir":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config/qmd","xdgCacheHome":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-cache"}
{"agentId":"trump","mode":"generated","configPath":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json","imports":[],"command":"~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd","args":["mcp"],"xdgConfigHome":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config","qmdConfigDir":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config/qmd","xdgCacheHome":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-cache"}

Autoreview:
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.87)
```

**Observed result after fix:** The manager classified the absolute-command mcporter `qmd` server as `generated` for both agents, wrote separate per-agent mcporter configs, preserved the absolute qmd command and `["mcp"]` args, and injected per-agent `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, `QMD_CONFIG_DIR`, and `XDG_CACHE_HOME` values.

**What was not tested:** I did not run the full product `openclaw memory ...` CLI in this linked checkout because the local user config is currently invalid on this branch and plugin commands do not register before config validation. I also did not test a live remote HTTP mcporter server; that preservation path remains covered by the focused unit tests already in this PR.

target.title: fix(browser): bring tab to foreground before screenshot capture
target.body:
## Summary

**What problem does this PR solve?**
`browser screenshot` (and the screenshot-backed snapshot path) hang until the CDP socket times out (~20s) whenever the target tab is **backgrounded**.

**Why does this matter now?**
On a multi-tab or node-hosted browser session, any capture of a non-foreground tab blocks for the full timeout, making screenshots effectively unusable without manual tab juggling.

**What is the intended outcome?**
Captures return promptly regardless of which tab is foregrounded.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**
The pure-a11y snapshot path (`Accessibility.getFullAXTree`) — it needs no rendered frame and already works on backgrounded tabs, so it is left untouched.

**What does success look like?**
A capture of a backgrounded tab returns in ~100ms instead of timing out.

**What should reviewers focus on?**
That issuing `Page.bringToFront` before capture is acceptable behavior (it activates the target tab). It is a no-op for a single-tab session and for an already-foregrounded tab, and its failure is swallowed so capture paths that already work cannot regress.

### Root cause
`Page.captureScreenshot {fromSurface:true}` waits for a compositor frame to commit. Under `--headless=new`, a **backgrounded tab never commits a new frame**, so the call blocks until timeout. `Accessibility.getFullAXTree` is unaffected (no rendered frame needed) — which is why text/a11y reads succeed while pixel capture hangs. The capture path issued no `Page.bringToFront`/`Target.activateTarget` before capturing.

### The fix
In `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.ts` `captureScreenshot()`, issue `await send("Page.bringToFront").catch(() => {})` immediately after `Page.enable` and before capture. Activating the tab forces a compositor frame to commit.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related # — none upstream; tracked downstream.
Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No — external contribution.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `Page.captureScreenshot` hangs on backgrounded tabs under headless Chrome.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw browser node pod, Chromium `Chrome/148.0.7778.215` headless (`--headless=new --no-sandbox --disable-gpu --disable-dev-shm-usage`), ARM64, driven via CDP on the live debugger port.
- **Exact steps run:** Over the browser's CDP endpoint, created two tabs (data-URL pages), activated the second so the first is backgrounded, attached to the backgrounded tab, then timed `Page.captureScreenshot` **before** vs **after** `Page.bringToFront` on the same session. Repeated 3x.
- **Evidence after fix (the mechanism this patch applies):**

  | run | backgrounded, no bringToFront | after `Page.bringToFront` |
  |-----|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
  | 1   | 3367 ms                       | **109 ms** |
  | 2   | 3222 ms                       | **84 ms**  |
  | 3   | 3260 ms                       | **117 ms** |

- **Observed result after fix:** Capture returns in ~100ms after `bringToFront`, vs multi-second (up to the full ~20s timeout on heavier real pages) without it.
- **What was not tested:** I verified the exact CDP behavior the patch applies (bringToFront → capture) directly against a live headless browser; I did not additionally rebuild and run a full OpenClaw image end-to-end through the `browser` tool. The patch inserts precisely the call that was measured.
- **Proof limitations:** A lightweight `data:` page degrades to ~3.3s rather than the full 20s a heavy page exhibits; the invariant (bringToFront → ~100ms) is identical in both, so before/after on the same tab is the load-bearing evidence.

## Tests and validation

- Targeted typecheck of the browser extension; the change is type-neutral (error-count delta vs the untouched base is 0 — the monorepo workspace deps are unbuilt in my checkout, so absolute-zero tsc isn't attainable there, but base == patched).
- No new automated test added: the failure mode is a headless-Chrome compositor timing behavior that requires a real backgrounded tab to reproduce; covered by the real-behavior proof above.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Yes** — capturing now foregrounds the target tab first.
- Did config/env/migration behavior change? **No.**
- Did security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution behavior change? **No.**
- Highest-risk area: `bringToFront` changes which tab is foreground in a multi-tab session.
- Mitigation: no-op for single-tab/already-foregrounded sessions; `.catch(() => {})` tolerates failure so existing working captures cannot regress.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: maintainer.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet (initial submission).

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit
Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
target.author: alaleiwang
